what:
OpenFn adaptor for Stripe payment operations. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to Stripe API.

It exposes 2 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. 21 ready-to-paste snippets are grouped as create (13) and search (8). Vendor homepage: https://stripe.com.
connect:
Stripe supports bearer tokens or API keys for API access.

The credential schema asks for baseUrl (required), apiVersion, and apiKey (required). Point the credential at the base URL: https://api.stripe.com/v1. Auth reference: https://docs.stripe.com/api/authentication.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in Stripe API.

17 triggers are catalogued (0 webhook, 17 polling), including Balance Transaction, Charge Refunded, Checkout Session Completed, Customer Subscription Updated, and more. See the Triggers tab for the full list. Stripe can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://docs.stripe.com/webhooks), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Kick the workflow off from a trigger on the Triggers tab (webhook-driven where supported, polling otherwise). Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Stripe. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Stripe jobs at volume:

- Prefer Stripe webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Paginate list and search calls explicitly; never assume a single response is the full result set.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: Bearer auth uses the secret API key directly.
best:
- Store Stripe credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-stripe to v1.0.10 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Validate inbound trigger payloads against the schemas on the Data tab before transforming them; payloads can drift between vendor versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
